# Questions in Livestream 50

Live Stream #50: Terminator Edition.

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/VeCTayU7MpQ?t=369" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">6:09</a> | What laws did Egyptians and Israelites have regarding grave desecration, and were they similar? | He says the two cultures handled grave desecration differently: Israelite concerns were mainly purity-related, while Egyptian concerns centered on tomb robbery and social order. | He distinguishes Israelite purity law from Egyptian custom. In Israelite practice, corpses and corpse-contact made a person ritually unclean, uncleanness could be transmitted to others, and later bone collection happened only after decay. In Egypt, bodies were ritually purified and could be handled by priests, while the major concern was tomb robbery and disruption of good order rather than a legal code against "grave desecration" as such. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/VeCTayU7MpQ?t=791" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">13:11</a> | Was organized crime a thing in ancient Egypt and the ancient Near East? | He says organized criminal bands are clearer in the wider ancient Near East, while Egyptian crime was more often theft or loosely organized tomb robbery. | He answers "yes and no": Mesopotamia and Syria had more movement between towns, so bands or leaders could organize robbers against travelers. Egypt, by contrast, had less mobility and more localized communities, so theft was common but usually one-off or loosely communal, such as tomb robbery at Deir el-Medina, not a mafia-like structure. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/VeCTayU7MpQ?t=967" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">16:07</a> | What is your view of biblical inerrancy, and is it older than Luther? | He says the concept predates Luther, affirms inerrancy with caveats about autographs and interpretation, and generally follows the Chicago Statement. | He says traces of inerrancy are found before Luther in the church fathers, but he defines his own view carefully: Scripture is accurate in what it affirms in the autographs, while acknowledging that the autographs are reconstructed from witnesses and that determining what the Bible affirms already involves fallible interpretation. He affirms inerrancy with contextual and hermeneutical caveats and says he generally follows the Chicago Statement. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/VeCTayU7MpQ?t=1241" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">20:41</a> | In Matthew 16:28 and 26:24, can the language refer to movement or ascension rather than only the Second Coming? | He says Matthew 16:28 may point to the kingdom's inauguration and ascension, while Matthew 26:24 uses a different verb in the context of betrayal and crucifixion. | He explains that the Matthew 16:28 verb can mean movement from one point to another, with the perspective of the narrator shaping whether it is rendered "come," "arrive," or even "go." He treats Matthew 16:28 as debated but more likely about inaugural eschatology, kingdom inauguration, and the ascension rather than a promise that someone present would live until the final Second Coming. For Matthew 26:24, he notes a different verb, meaning to depart or go out, in the betrayal and crucifixion context. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/VeCTayU7MpQ?t=1688" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">28:08</a> | What actually happened in Genesis 9:20-27 with Ham and Noah? | He says "uncovering nakedness" is a euphemism for a serious sexual offense involving Noah's wife, while noting the text leaves some details unclear. | He reads "uncovering your father's nakedness" as an Israelite euphemism for sleeping with the father's wife, not merely seeing Noah unclothed. He says Ham is portrayed as proud enough of the act to tell his brothers, and he connects the severity of the curse to Israelite concerns about incestuous or family-boundary violations. He keeps uncertainty where the text is silent, including whether Noah's wife was Ham's mother and whether Canaan's curse implies Canaan came from that act. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/VeCTayU7MpQ?t=2092" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">34:52</a> | Does an Egyptian absence record mentioning "drinking with Khonsu" refer to a festival? | He says it most likely refers to a drinking festival connected with Khonsu, though the exact festival calendar is not known. | He identifies the note as a festival absence from Deir el-Medina-style day records, where worker absences and reasons were recorded. He compares it to other Egyptian religious drinking festivals, including epagomenal days and the festival of Bastet, and says "drinking with Khonsu" points to a drinking festival for the moon god Khonsu. The exact festival cannot be pinned down because not every temple's festival calendar survives. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/VeCTayU7MpQ?t=2319" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">38:39</a> | Did ancient Egyptians use sunscreen? | He says not in the modern sense; they did use cosmetics such as eye paint to reduce glare and adjusted work patterns around heat. | He says typical Egyptians did not use sunscreen in a modern sense, though he allows that substances such as ochre are possible without strong evidence. The clearer evidence is that men and women used cosmetics, especially eye paint, partly to reduce glare, and perfumes and cosmetics occur in tombs of both sexes. He also notes practical heat adaptation: people avoided the hottest parts of the day and the height of summer for some work. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/VeCTayU7MpQ?t=2607" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">43:27</a> | How should the `tanninim hagadolim` of Genesis 1:21 be identified? | He rejects dinosaurs, dragons, and whales, and sees the phrase as ancient Near Eastern sea-monster or deep-serpent imagery. | He rejects dinosaur, dragon, and whale identifications, arguing that the term belongs in an ancient Near Eastern mythological register. He links the imagery to monsters of the deep, such as Leviathan, Yam, and Mot, and says these are the sorts of beings Israelites imagined in the deepest parts of the sea. He glosses the phrase as something like "the great monster-serpents" rather than a modern biological category. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/VeCTayU7MpQ?t=2827" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">47:07</a> | Why was bovine worship so widespread across the ancient world? | He points to cattle's importance for agrarian life, including milk, meat, leather, plowing, fertilizer, and cleaner farming around water supplies. | He says bovine veneration was widespread because cattle were exceptionally useful in agrarian societies. They supplied milk, meat, cheese, leather, plowing power, and fertilizer, and he argues they were cleaner around waterways than sheep, goats, or pigs in contexts where irrigation and drinking water mattered. Because they supported soil, food, and water management, he describes them as "miracle animals" that could easily become almost divine in ancient agricultural cultures. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/VeCTayU7MpQ?t=3146" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">52:26</a> | How should I respond when young-earth creationists call someone a heretic for doubting a 6,000-year-old earth? | He says the age of the earth is not an essential of the Christian faith and should not be treated as heresy. | He says the age of the earth has never been an essential doctrine in church history and should not divide Christians. He defines heresy narrowly as error affecting salvation or the identity of God, especially after warning and refusal to be corrected, and says disagreement over a 6,000-year-old earth does not fit that category. His suggested response is that young-earth chronology is a doctrine of man added alongside the gospel, not the gospel itself. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/VeCTayU7MpQ?t=3539" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">58:59</a> | If Jesus and Joshua have the same Greek name, why are both not called Jesus in English? | He says English preserves two translational traditions: Joshua from Hebrew and Jesus through Greek and Latin, partly to distinguish Jesus Christ from other Joshuas. | He explains that both names go back to forms of Yehoshua, but English received them through different translation histories: Joshua from the Hebrew form and Jesus through Greek and Latin. He says English could have rendered both the same way, but inherited idiosyncratic traditional renderings, and the distinction also helps separate Jesus Christ from other people named Joshua or Iesous in ancient sources. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/VeCTayU7MpQ?t=3769" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:02:49</a> | Did Moses think he could gain power over God by knowing God's secret name at the burning bush? | He says that idea fits Egyptian magic generally, but the Moses narrative presents Moses as God's servant rather than someone manipulating God. | He says someone raised in Egypt could know the magical idea that a true divine name might be used to manipulate a god, and he connects that background with the command not to take the Lord's name in vain as including magical misuse or misrepresentation. But he does not see Moses doing that in the burning bush narrative: Moses appears overwhelmed by God and presented as God's servant, not as someone trying to make God serve him. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/VeCTayU7MpQ?t=3965" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:06:05</a> | Is Genesis 1:1 better translated "In the beginning God created" or "When God began to create"? | He prefers "In the beginning God created," saying the subordinate-clause translation rests on shaky linguistic grounds even if verse 1 can function as a title. | He prefers the straightforward translation "In the beginning God created the heavens and the earth." He says turning verse 1 into a subordinate clause is linguistically shaky, while the Hebrew can still allow verse 1 to function as a title or introductory statement because verse 2 has a conjunction and a syntactical shift rather than a simple consecutive continuation. For him, it is clearer to keep the direct translation and explain the titular function exegetically. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/VeCTayU7MpQ?t=4502" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:15:02</a> | Is the Tale of Sinuhe connected with the story of Joseph? | He says the similarities are probably coincidence; Sinuhe moves from Egypt to the Levant, Joseph's setting is later, and Sinuhe may reflect a real tomb-biography tradition. | He says the connection is probably coincidental and even opposite in direction: Sinuhe leaves Egypt for the Levant after a royal assassination, while Joseph's story has a later setting and different movement. He places Sinuhe in the Twelfth Dynasty and Joseph closer to a Second Intermediate Period context, perhaps about two centuries later. He also notes that he is not in the majority, but he suspects Sinuhe may preserve or imitate a real tomb-biography tradition rather than pure fiction. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/VeCTayU7MpQ?t=4945" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:22:25</a> | What are your views on the persons of Adam and Eve? | He says he regards Adam and Eve as historical people and archetypes, with Adam also functioning as a clan progenitor in the narrative. | He says Adam and Eve were real historical people, but also archetypes: real persons given a mytho-historical function as the first people. He thinks Adam functioned as a clan progenitor, so Adam's long lifespan may represent the memory or continuity of his clan rather than one individual's biological age. He also affirms the Eden setting, the forbidden fruit, and Adam and Eve as the cause of original sin. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/VeCTayU7MpQ?t=5106" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:25:06</a> | What kind of governments in Canaan did Israel go against? | He says Canaan was made up largely of small city-states ruled by kings or chiefs, not one unified empire or nation. | He uses the Amarna letters, from about two centuries before the conquest in his framing, as the best comparative picture. Canaanite settlements were small, depopulated city-states, each with its own king or chief, often fighting one another even under Egyptian imperial influence. Some stronger cities such as Hazor could dominate surrounding towns, but he does not describe Canaan as one unified empire or nation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/VeCTayU7MpQ?t=5389" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:29:49</a> | Is Mark 14:62 also about the ascension? | He says yes, treating it as another ascension-related text. | He answers briefly and directly that Mark 14:62 is also about the ascension. The transcript gives no extended argument at this point, but he explicitly connects the passage to the same ascension reading discussed earlier for similar Son of Man language. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/VeCTayU7MpQ?t=5447" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:30:47</a> | Were ladders used in some Egyptian art for Osiris, and is there a Jacob's ladder connection? | He says Egyptians certainly had and used ladders, including for tomb and temple work, but he is unsure about the specific Osiris-art claim. | He is unsure what specific Osiris-art claim the question refers to, but says Egyptians certainly had ladders and used them to reach and decorate high tomb and temple walls. He gives examples of tomb construction and decorated high walls where ladders would be necessary. In a follow-up, he says the djed pillar might possibly be understood as a kind of ladder or as Osiris's spine analogically, but he cannot say for sure. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/VeCTayU7MpQ?t=5756" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:35:56</a> | Does the Greek myth of Io relate to Hathor? | He says it is possible because some Greek myth has Egyptian connections, but he does not know Greek myth well enough to answer confidently. | He explicitly limits his answer because Greek myth is not his specialty. He says it is possible, since some Greek myth does seem to have Egyptian connections and the proposed cow/Hathor link would not surprise him, but he does not know the Greek myth of Io or the history of Greek myth well enough to affirm the connection. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/VeCTayU7MpQ?t=5895" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:38:15</a> | What do you think of the claim that Daniel is a 165 BCE forgery? | He rejects that dating, saying the argument often assumes prophecy cannot happen and that objections to Daniel's historicity have possible explanations. | He does not accept the 165 BCE forgery claim and says much of the dating argument is driven by the assumption that successful prophecy must be ex eventu. He allows that prophetic material can be recontextualized across generations without becoming a forgery. He also raises the Septuagint and possible explanations for alleged anachronisms, while admitting he has not done deep work on Daniel and can only speak cautiously. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/VeCTayU7MpQ?t=6255" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:44:15</a> | Is Kathleen Kenyon's dating of Jericho's destruction layer accurate, and was it disturbed by erosion or site leveling? | He says Kenyon's dating of City IV's middle bronze destruction layer is solid, while later bronze layers were more affected by erosion or leveling. | He says Kenyon's dating of City IV's Middle Bronze fortifications to about 1550 BCE is solid and based on ceramic typology. He distinguishes that from later Late Bronze remains, which were more affected by erosion and site leveling, including wind erosion and leveling above a Late Bronze 1A wall. He doubts that Middle Bronze stratigraphy was disturbed enough to invalidate Kenyon's result, while also saying that layer is not a good fit for the Jericho conquest narrative. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/VeCTayU7MpQ?t=6611" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:50:11</a> | Is Jacob's ladder Egyptian? | He says it could be, but it does not need to be, since Canaanites and Israelites also knew and used ladders. | He says it is hard to know and could be Egyptian, but there is no need to make that move because Canaanites and Israelites were already familiar with ladders. He points to watchtowers and two-story Semitic houses where ladders would be used to move between floors, with livestock below and sleeping space above. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/VeCTayU7MpQ?t=6716" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:51:56</a> | Was Seth the biological son of Adam or a later progenitor from Adam's clan? | He says Seth could be a progenitor in Adam's line, because ancient Israelite father-son language could span generations. | He leans toward Seth being a progenitor within Adam's clan rather than necessarily Adam's direct biological son. He explains that ancient Israelite kinship language could call ancestors across several generations "father" and descendants "sons" or "daughters," so generational distance would not break the father-son relationship. On that basis, Seth could be in Adam's line and still be called Adam's son. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/VeCTayU7MpQ?t=6926" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:55:26</a> | Did you have a good May 4th, and is there any new Egyptology news? | He says no specific new Egyptology news comes to mind, then notes the channel has reached its second anniversary. | He says no specific new Egyptology news comes to mind, then adds an announcement that the channel had reached its second anniversary because its first video was published on May 5. He frames it as a birthday milestone rather than a scholarly news update. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/VeCTayU7MpQ?t=7073" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:57:53</a> | Would nuclear war mean the end times would have to start during such a war? | He says not necessarily; nuclear weapons are devastating, especially to cities, but should not automatically be read as starting an end-times scenario. | He says nuclear war would not necessarily start the end times. He emphasizes that nuclear weapons are devastating especially against cities, where debris and urban density amplify destruction, but a weapon used in an isolated field would not have the same effect. Even tactical nuclear use, in his view, would not automatically trigger an end-times scenario; much more would have to happen, so people should not read too much eschatology into it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/VeCTayU7MpQ?t=7375" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:02:55</a> | Where should a layman start to learn more about Akhenaten and Atenism? | He recommends Aidan Dodson's `Amarna Sunset` and mentions a forthcoming article, while disagreeing with the view that Atenism was monotheism. | He recommends Aidan Dodson's `Amarna Sunset` as the best book he has read on Akhenaten and Atenism. He also mentions that he has a forthcoming Oxford article on Akhenaten, Amarna, and Atenism, and contrasts his view with James Hoffmeier's more conventional monotheism reading, which he says he does not accept. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/VeCTayU7MpQ?t=7694" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:08:14</a> | Can we identify which Egyptian deity each plague is polemicizing against? | He says no; he thinks the plague-deity matching view overreads Exodus 12:12 and that the plagues are judgment on Pharaoh rather than a deity-by-deity polemic. | He rejects the deity-by-deity matching approach, saying it shoehorns Egyptian gods into the plagues and collapses quickly. He says Exodus 12:12 is the only verse tying the plagues to judgment on Egypt's gods, and even there the verse is about the tenth plague and may refer to those with power over life and death rather than the entire pantheon. He treats the plagues primarily as judgment on Pharaoh, the king, and those supporting his tyranny. |
